data.title: White House Press Secretary Levitt says tariffs on China have not gone into effect yet
data.description:
White House Press Secretary Levitt says tariffs on China have not gone into effect yet. The 104% cumulative tariffs on China will go into effect at midnight tonight (they have not gone into effect).

The initial report was hosted by Edward Lawrence from Fox news:

Meanwhile Apple is loading a 747 with 200,000 iPhones.

Drax Group plc

Transaction in own shares

Drax Group plc ("Drax Group") announces that, in accordance with the terms of its share buyback programme (the "Programme") announced on 7 August 2024, it has purchased the following number of its ordinary shares of 11 16/29 pence each ("Ordinary Shares") through RBC Europe Limited.

08 April 2025

58,165

554.51

Following the above transaction, Drax Group will have 429,591,344 Ordinary Shares in issue (including treasury shares). The total number of Ordinary Shares held in treasury is 70,791,045.

The total number of voting rights in Drax Group, excluding treasury shares as at 08 April 2025 is 358,800,299. This figure may be used by shareholders (and others with notification obligations) as the denominator for the calculations by which they will determine if they are required to notify their interest in, or a change to their interest in, Drax Group under the FCA's Disclosure and Transparency Rules.

Since the announcement of the Programme on 7 August 2024, Drax Group has purchased 30,818,053 Ordinary Shares in aggregate for treasury.

data.title: UN chief rejects new Israeli plan to control Gaza aid
data.description:
United Nations Secretary-General Antonio Guterres on Tuesday rejected a new Israeli proposal to control aid deliveries in Gaza, saying it risks "further controlling and callously limiting aid down to the last calorie and grain of flour."

"Let me be clear: We will not participate in any arrangement that does not fully respect the humanitarian principles: humanity, impartiality, independence and neutrality," Guterres told reporters.

No aid has been delivered to the Palestinian enclave of some 2.1 million people since March 2. Israel has said it would not allow the entry of all goods and supplies into Gaza until Palestinian militants Hamas release all remaining hostages.

COGAT, the Israeli military agency that coordinates aid, last week met with U.N. agencies and international aid groups and said it proposed "a structured monitoring and aid entry mechanism" for Gaza.

"The mechanism is designed to support aid organizations, enhance oversight and accountability, and ensure that assistance reaches the civilian population in need, rather than being diverted and stolen by Hamas," COGAT posted on X on Sunday.

Jonathan Whittall, the senior U.N. aid official for Gaza and the West Bank, said last week that there was no evidence of aid being diverted.

Israel last month resumed its bombardment of Gaza after a two-month truce and sent troops back into the enclave.

"Gaza is a killing field – and civilians are in an endless death loop," said Guterres as he again called for the immediate and unconditional release of all hostages, a permanent ceasefire, and full humanitarian access in Gaza.

"With crossing points into Gaza shut and aid blockaded, security is in shambles and our capacity to deliver has been strangled," he said.

"As the occupying power, Israel has unequivocal obligations under international law – including international humanitarian law and international human rights law," Guterres said.

That means Israel should facilitate relief programs and ensure food, medical care, hygiene and public-health standards in Gaza, he said. "None of that is happening today," he added.

Israel says it does not exercise effective control over Gaza and therefore is not an occupying power.

The war in Gaza was triggered on Oct. 7, 2023, when Hamas killed 1,200 people in southern Israel, and took some 250 hostages, according to Israeli tallies. Since then, more than 50,000 Palestinians have been killed, according to Gaza health authorities.

data.title: Nymex Overview: Petroleum Futures Up at Midday, Below Morning HighsOPIS
data.description:
Crude oil futures were higher near midday Tuesday in continued volatile trading that earlier left Brent and West Texas Intermediate futures in the red.

Petroleum markets may have found some support from equity markets that opened Tuesday's session with a strong rally. While stock indexes were higher at near midday, the gains were weakening some in brisk trading.

WTI volume over the last three days has averaged roughly 1.85 million contracts and trading volume on Monday topped 2 million lots for the first time since mid-January.

The NYMEX May WTI and June Brent contracts were slightly higher heading into the afternoon. The May WTI was up 39cts to $61.09/bbl just after noon ET and June Brent was 12cts higher at $64.33/bbl.

Gasoline and distillate contracts were up by 1ct to 2cts, leaving them a couple of cents below morning highs. The May ULSD was about 2cts higher at $2.0895/gal and the NYMEX May RBOB contract was 1.29cts higher at $2.033/gal.

Cash markets were mixed, despite the gains in futures. The San Francisco CARBOB market continued to cool off from earlier highs. East of the Rockies markets were mostly following futures, but wider discounts in Gulf Coast gasoline left prices there relatively flat.

data.title: Netflix Looks Insulated From a Trade War4 Reasons Why.
data.description:
By Adam Levine

Though it faces risk like any international firm these days, Netflix may be the tech company that can skate through a trade war with the least amount of damage.

In contrast to product-makers like Apple, down 16% since President Donald Trump announced his latest tariffs last week, Netflix is down 3%. The S&P 500 is off 9% over the same stretch.

Unlike many big tech companies, Netflix is integrated into local economies around the world. Punishing the streaming platform may turn out to be unattractive.

Netflix still faces the same larger threat as everyone else — that a trade war will sink the global economy into a deep recession. On Monday at the Economic Club of New York, BlackRock CEO Larry Fink said that "most CEOs I talk to would say we are probably in a recession right now."

Netflix would likely see some subscription loss in a global recession, but inexpensive entertainment has traditionally survived and even flourished in economic downturns. The Great Depression was considered by many to be the "Golden Age of Hollywood."

"Staying home watching Netflix is definitely a cheaper way to spend the night than going out to a restaurant or going to a venue," Oppenheimer analyst Jason Helfstein told Barron's.

But investors have to also consider the threat of retaliation in an escalating trade war. U.S. goods are squarely in the crosshairs of other countries, and countermeasures may include what is known as "digital service taxes." Netflix already faces these in several countries, along with additional requirements to invest in local film production. The taxes are generally in the range of 2% to 5% of revenue. In a trade war, foreign countries could raise these rates, while other nations could impose new ones.

But subscription-based Netflix may get different treatment from ad-based offerings like Alphabet's Google Search and Meta's Facebook. High taxes on advertising may make their way to consumer prices, but it is hidden to them as one cost among many — and thus more politically expedient.

With subscriptions, though, a digital tax would result in direct price increases, and Netflix would surely communicate the reason for the hike. That makes digital subscription taxes a domestic political liability.

Countries could also impose new local investment requirements, but such a move could actually protect Netflix, which already spends significantly in local economies. From 2020 to 2023, according to Bernstein analyst Laurent Yoon, Netflix invested $6.8 billion in Europe and maintained 11 local offices and production hubs.

Netflix also licenses a lot of foreign content, according to Yoon; 60% of all titles on Netflix are from non-U.S. sources. "No other platform comes close to what Netflix does," Yoon said. These licensing fees to local producers are another reason countries may be wary of punishing Netflix.

Netflix could even be protected from international boycotts of U.S. products. The company is the number one streamer in almost every market where it competes, and it will be hard to replace it with local alternatives. Even in France, where the government expends a lot of effort protecting local culture, French Canal Plus has a far smaller subscriber base than Netflix, according to Yoon.

Write to Adam Levine at adam.levine@barrons.com

data.title: Gasoline margins rise
data.description:
Northwest European gasoline refining profit margins rose by a little over $1 a barrel on Tuesday, as April exports out of Europe are set for a rise month-on-month.

  • A total of 10,000 metric tons of Eurobob E5 traded, with BP, Gunvor and Vitol selling to Litasco and Musket.

  • Just 1,000 tons of Eurobob E10 traded with Phillips 66 selling to BP.

  • Wider gasoline-naphtha spreads are opening up various arbitrage opportunities for gasoline blenders in ARA, according to Sparta Commodities' Philip Jones-Lux, including to Mexico and Canada that would usually be serviced by the U.S. Gulf coast refining hub.

  • EU and UK gasoline and blending component exports to other regions are set to jump to 1.14 million barrels per day in April, up from 860,000 bpd in March, Kpler data shows.

  • Venezuela's state-run oil company PDVSA is restarting its El Palito refinery's fluid catalytic cracker, which remained shut for 11 months, to partially offset the suspension of key units at its largest refinery due to a blackout, sources close to its operations said on Tuesday.​

1251 ET - Deutsche Bank analyst Michael Linenberg expects a "mild" airline recession with an 8% reduction in top-line, less than prior recessions such as the 16% drop in 2008 and roughly 70% drop during Covid. Linenberg predicts sales will bottom during the second half of 2025, staying at a low for about two quarters given the nature and timing of the slowdown. In prior recessions, the industry's trough period has been considerably longer, usually lasting a minimum of four quarters, he said. Some demand impacts will extend into 2026, he said, and industry pretax profits for 2025 will decline by about 40%.(katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1245 ET - UBS thinks Levi Strauss is better positioned than most to deal with tariffs because of its global supply chain network. In a research note, analysts also say the company continues to execute well on factors within its control and just had one of its best quarters over the past few years driven by better-than-anticipated revenue growth and gross margins. Overall, Levi's continuing transformation into a global, lifestyle brand for both men and women should continue to fuel share gains over the long term, the analysts add. UBS raises its FY25 EPS estimate by about 3% and leaves its FY26 and FY27 EPS views unchanged. Levi Strauss falls 5% to $12.82, and is off 25% year-to-date. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1201 ET - Gold futures recoup some lost ground as safe-haven demand grows. Futures are up 1.5% at $3,019 a troy ounce, though they sit down 4.2% on week after investors liquidated positions in prior sessions to cover losses elsewhere in the market. Despite the short-lived downward correction, the precious metal is emerging as a first-rate asset, ActivTrades' Ion Jauregui says in a note. Tariff policies are set to generate inflation and increase economic uncertainty, forcing banks and large investors to rethink their strategies. This should drive up interest in gold as a refuge against the devaluation of other assets, Jauregui says. The convergence of factors including tariff uncertainty, rising inflation and intensifying geopolitical concerns suggests gold could reach, or even exceed $4,000 a troy ounce as the increasing money supply pushes the valuation of the metal, Jauregui adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

1146 ET - The semiconductor sector will likely be hit by the uncertainty from U.S. tariffs, Jefferies analysts Janardan Menon and Om Bakhda write in a note. "Chip suppliers should meet first quarter estimates, but their second quarter and 2025 outlooks are likely to be impacted," they add. The industry will face supply-chain disruption and weak demand, with sales expected to decelerate faster than previously anticipated from April, they say. "Global semiconductor growth rates [are expected] to dip below 10% by the end of this year, with the likelihood of going into negative territory in first half 2026. As a result, global semiconductor revenues could potentially decline next year," they say. ASML Holding and ASM International shares are up 4.2% and 2.7%, respectively. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

1126 ET - Levi Strauss' latest results checked all the boxes Barclays analysts were looking for, including continued acceleration in wholesale and a significant gross expansion. But the fate of its stock will be dictated by tariffs and how the apparel retailer responds to it through pricing, the analysts say. "Given the breadth of incremental tariffs across most points of origins, the exact exposure doesn't mean much — prices will need to increase, it's only a question of when, where, and by how much," the analysts say. Either way, Levi is well positioned, the analysts say. Barclays cuts its price target on the stock to $18 a share from $22. Levi shares fall 2.7% to $13.14. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1124 ET - Adidas is set to report decent first-quarter results, although U.S. tariffs will be in the spotlight, Warburg Research analysts write in a note. All major sourcing countries are subject to Trump's levies, including Vietnam, the country to which the industry pivoted to escape higher wage costs and tariff threats in China, the analyst says. Sporting-goods companies could raise prices in the U.S. to mitigate the impact of duties. While there could be a U.S. recession, Adidas should be able to win market share and return to double-digit earnings margins, the analyst says. "Outside the U.S., we expect more fiscal stimulus in Europe and China, which should counterbalance the hit," he adds. The analyst upgrades the rating for the stock to buy from hold. Shares are up 2.8% at 191.65 euros. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

1116 ET - Stantec's tuck-in acquisition of Ryan Hanley expands its Ireland footprint and strengthens its capabilities in the water sector. TD Cowen's Michael Tupholme says this has been a focus for the company since its 2023 investor day when "management highlighted its desire to build on Stantec's Ireland presence." He figures that RH generates annual net revenue in the mid-C$20 million range which could contribute Ebitda of C$3.6 million. "We see the deal as aligned with Stantec's overall growth strategy and expect it to strengthen its presence and capabilities in the Irish water sector," he says. Shares are up 1.1% to C$115.05. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1055 ET - Stocks in the consumer discretionary space have been hit hard by the recent market selloff, but the heavily shorted sector now seems primed for a short squeeze, and even some bargain hunting for long-term investors, Morgan Stanley analysts say in a research note. They're still underweight on the sector overall, and have been for the past three and a half years. But last Friday, the sector outperformed as traders took advantage of the high short interest, the analysts say. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1047 ET - The stock market has been telling us for almost a year that growth was going to slow, and since January, it's been warning of an even greater slowdown with recession risks rising, Morgan Stanley analysts say in a research note. Cyclical stocks started underperforming in April of last year and are now down more than 40% compared with defensive stocks, the analysts say. Cyclicals have underperformed defensives to a degree that previously was only seen during recessions, not prior to them, they say. The analysts believe unprecedented fiscal spending, AI buildout costs and wealthy consumer spending have masked weakness in the private economy. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1031 ET - The stock market has upside potential if cooler heads prevail when it comes to negotiations on trade around tariffs, addressing the need to cut deficit spending and finding solutions for reducing national debt, Oppenheimer Asset Management directors say in a note. They remain overweight on U.S. equities and maintain a meaningful exposure to developed and emerging markets on expectations that U.S. economic growth will boost activity around the world. Exposure to large-cap companies that do business overseas can offer exposure to international markets, and a further weakening in the dollar could boost the results of their foreign operations, they say. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1025 ET - Stocks rebound after Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent says tariff negotiations could lead to good deals for the U.S. The tariff announcements last week have driven up the risk of a recession and led to a market selloff. But Morgan Stanley analysts say they could form a floor if President Trump delays the April 9 effective date for implementing additional tariffs beyond the initial 10% levy that went into effect last weekend. Prices could also be primed for a rebound if a number of nations, including Europe, come to the table and negotiate on terms that are favorable to the U.S., the analysts say. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1009 ET - The steep pullback in Apple's stock price presents "a particularly enhanced buying opportunity for investors to own a high-quality name," says Bank of America. In a note, BofA says the risk/reward for Apple has skewed positive when its forward price-to-earnings multiple falls below 25x, and that the drop in Apple's share price through Monday's session has pushed its P/E multiple to around 21x. BofA reiterates its buy rating on Apple shares, citing stable cash flows, earnings resiliency and the potential benefits of AI use on edge devices. Apple up 3.2% to $187.19. (colin.kellaher@wsj.com)

data.title: REG - Tandem Grp PLC - Grant of Options & Surrender of Existing Options
data.description:
RNS Number : 2021E Tandem Group PLC 08 April 2025  

 

8 April 2025

Tandem Group plc

(the "Company")

Grant of Share Options, Surrender of Existing Options & PDMR Dealings

Tandem Group plc (AIM: TND), designers, developers, distributors and retailers of sports, leisure and mobility equipment, announces that Peter Kimberley, Chief Executive Officer, David Poulter, Chief Commercial Officer, and Gurvinder Kaur, Chief Financial Officer and Company Secretary of the Company, have today been granted 38,000, 20,000 and 20,000 options respectively over ordinary shares of 25 pence each in the share capital of the Company ("Ordinary Shares") under the 2019 Employee Share Option Scheme.

The Options have an exercise price of 161.50 pence per ordinary share, being the closing middle market price per ordinary share on 7 April 2025. The Options are exercisable between 1 January 2028 and 7 April 2035, after which the Options will lapse.

A further 91,000 options have been granted to other employees under the 2019 Employee Share Option Scheme.

The Company further announces that on 7 April 2025, Peter Kimberley, Chief Executive Officer of the Company, has surrendered 37,500 share options with an exercise price of 325.0 pence.

The notification set out below, made in accordance with the requirements of Article 19 of the Market Abuse Regulation, provides further details of such dealings.
